volumeTitle: The Merchant. The Braggart Soldier. The Ghost. The Persian
label: The Persian (Section 1.1.47b - Section 1.3.147)

sections.label: Section 1.1.47b
sections.original: Si quid erit , te fac iam ut scias .
sections.translation: Still do you try to get it ; I ’ ll carefully do the same . If anything shall turn up , I ’ ll let you know at once .

sections.label: Section 1.1.48
sections.original: ⋯ obsecro te resecro que , operam da hánc mihi
sections.translation: I entreat you , and entreat over and over again , do give me your stanch help in this .

sections.label: Section 1.1.48ab
sections.translation: O dear ! you are worrying me to death by your importunity .

sections.label: Section 1.1.49
sections.original: Amóris vitio , non meo , nunc tibi morologus fio .
sections.translation: It ’ s through the fault of love , and not my own , that I ’ m now become a silly prater to you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.50
sections.original: At pol ego aps te concessero .—
sections.translation: Then , i ’ troth , I ’ ll now be taking my leave of you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.50b
sections.translation: Are you going away , then ? A good walk to you . But betake yourself back as soon as you can , and do take care that I haven ’ t to seek you ; I shall be close at home until I have cooked up a mishap for the Procurer .

sections.label: Section 1.2.53
sections.original: Veterem atque antiquom quaestum maio rum meum
sections.translation: The old and ancient calling of my forefathers do I follow , and hold , and cultivate with great care .

sections.label: Section 1.2.55
sections.original: nam numquam quisquam meorum maiorum fuit ,
sections.translation: For never was there any one of my forefathers , but that by acting the parasite they filled their bellies : my father , grandfather , great-grandfather , great-great-grandfather , his father , and his grandfather , just like mice , always fed on the victuals of others , and in love of good eating no one could excel them .

sections.label: Section 1.2.60
sections.original: atque eis cognómentum erat durís Capitonibus .
sections.translation: Hard Heads [*] was their surname . From them have I derived this calling , and the station of my forefathers ; nor do I wish myself to turn informer [*] , nor indeed does it become me , without risk of my own , to go seize upon the goods of other people ; nor do those persons please who do so ; I ’ m speaking out .

sections.label: Section 1.2.65
sections.original: nam publicae rei causa quicumque id facit
sections.translation: For whoever does this , more for the sake of the public than of his own benefit , my mind can be induced to believe that he is a citizen both faithful and deserving ; but if he should not prosecute to conviction the breaker of the laws , let him pay one half of the intended penalty to the public . And let this , too , be written in that law ;

sections.label: Section 1.2.70
sections.translation: when an informer has prosecuted any one , let the other in his turn [*] sue him for just as much , and upon equal terms let them come before the Triumvirs [*] . If that were done , assuredly I ’ d make those nowhere to be seen , who here with their whitened nets [*] lay siege to the property of others .

sections.label: Section 1.2.75
sections.original: sed sumne ego stultus , qui rem curo publicam ,
sections.translation: But am I not a simpleton , to be taking care of the public interests when there are the magistrates , whose duty it is to take care of them ? Now I ’ ll in-doors here ; I ’ ll go look after the scraps from yesterday , whether they have rested well or not ; whether they have had a fever [*] ; whether they ’ ve been well covered up or not , so that no one could creep up to them .

sections.label: Section 1.2.80
sections.translation: But the door is opening ; I must pause in my steps .

sections.label: Section 1.3.81
sections.original: Omnem rem inveni , ut sua sibi pecunia
sections.translation: I ’ ve hit upon the whole matter , so that with his own money the Procurer may this day make her his freed-woman . But see , here ’ s the Parasite whose assistance I have need of . I ’ ll make believe as though I didn ’ t see him ; in that way I ’ ll allure the fellow .

sections.label: Section 1.3.85
sections.original: curate istic vos atque adproperate ocius ,
sections.translation: ( Goes to the door , and calls to the SERVANTS within .) Do you attend , you there , and quickly make haste , that I mayn ’ t have any delay when I come in-doors . Mix the honied wine ; get ready the quinces and the junkets [*] , that they may be nicely warmed upon the dishes , and throw in some scented calamus [*] . I ’ faith , that boon-companion of mine , I fancy , will be here just now .

sections.label: Section 1.3.90
sections.translation: He ’ s meaning me — bravo !

sections.label: Section 1.3.90b
sections.translation: I think that he ’ ll be here just now from the baths when he has bathed .

sections.label: Section 1.3.91b
sections.translation: How he does keep everything in its due order .

sections.label: Section 1.3.92
sections.original: Collyrae facite ut madeant et colyphia ,
sections.translation: Take you care that the gravy-cakes [*] and the cheese-biscuits [*] are hot ; don ’ t be giving them to me unbaked .

sections.label: Section 1.3.93b
sections.translation: He ’ s speaking the very fact ; they are worth nothing raw , only if you swallow them warm .

sections.label: Section 1.3.95
sections.original: tum nisi cremore crassost ius collyricum ,
sections.translation: Then , unless the broth for the gravy-cakes is of a thick consistency , that miserable , thin , pale , transparent stuff , is worth nothing at all . The broth for a gravy-cake ought to be like a soup . I don ’ t want it to be going into my bladder , I want it for my stomach .

sections.label: Section 1.3.99
sections.original: Prope me hic nescio quis loquitur .
sections.translation: Some one , I know not who , is talking near me here .

sections.label: Section 1.3.99b
sections.translation: O my earthly Jupiter , your fellow-feaster addresses you .

sections.label: Section 1.3.101
sections.translation: O Saturio , you ’ ve come opportunely for me .

sections.label: Section 1.3.102
sections.original: Mendacium edepol dicis , atque haud te decet :
sections.translation: Upon my faith , you are telling a lie , and it becomes you not ; for as Hungerio [*] I ’ m come , not as Saturio am I come .

sections.label: Section 1.3.104
sections.original: At edes , nam iam intus ventris fumant focula .
sections.translation: But you shall have something to eat ; for now the creature-comforts for the stomach are smoking away in-doors .

sections.label: Section 1.3.105
sections.translation: I ’ ve ordered the remnants to be warmed .

sections.label: Section 1.3.105b
sections.translation: Why , it ’ s the proper thing for the gammon to be served up cold the day after .

sections.label: Section 1.3.107
sections.translation: I ’ ve ordered it so to be done .

sections.label: Section 1.3.107b
sections.translation: Any caviare [*] ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.107c
sections.translation: Get out — do you ask the question ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.108
sections.translation: You have a capital notion of what ’ s good [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.3.108b
sections.translation: But do you at all remember the matter about which I was making mention to you yesterday ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.110
sections.original: Memini : ut murena et conger ne calefierent ;
sections.translation: I recollect ; that the lamprey and the conger ought not to be made warm ; for they are much better stripped of their meat [*] when cold . But why do we delay to commence the engagement ? While it ’ s the morning , it befits all people to eat .

sections.label: Section 1.3.114
sections.translation: It ’ s almost too early in the morning .

sections.label: Section 1.3.114b
sections.translation: The business that you begin to do in the morning , that same lasts on throughout the day .

sections.label: Section 1.3.116
sections.original: Quaeso animum advorte hoc . iám heri narravi tibi
sections.translation: Prithee , do give your attention to this . For yesterday I mentioned it to you , and entreated you to lend me six hundred didrachms .

sections.label: Section 1.3.118b
sections.translation: I recollect it and am aware , both that you did ask me , and that I hadn ’ t any to lend .

sections.label: Section 1.3.120
sections.original: nihili parasitus est , cui árgentum domi est :
sections.translation: A Parasite ’ s good for nothing that has got money at home ; he has a longing at once to begin upon an entertainment , and to gobble away at his own expense , if he has anything at home . A Parasite ought to be a right down needy Cynic ; he ought to have a leather bottle [*] , a strigil , an utensil [*] , a pair of slippers , a cloak ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.125
sections.translation: and a purse ; and in that a little of the needful , with which he may just cheer up the existence of his own household .

sections.label: Section 1.3.127
sections.original: Iam nolo argentum : filiam utendam tuam
sections.translation: I don ’ t want money now ; lend me your daughter .

sections.label: Section 1.3.128b
sections.original: Numquam edepol cuiquam etiam utendam dedi .
sections.translation: By my troth , never to any person whatsoever have I lent her as yet .

sections.label: Section 1.3.129
sections.original: Non ad istuc quod tu insimulas .
sections.translation: Not for that purpose which you are insinuating .

sections.label: Section 1.3.129b
sections.translation: Why do you want her then ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.129c
sections.translation: You shall know ;

sections.label: Section 1.3.130
sections.original: quia forma lepida et liberali est .
sections.translation: because she ’ s of a pretty and genteel figure .

sections.label: Section 1.3.130b
sections.translation: Such is the fact .

sections.label: Section 1.3.131
sections.original: Hic leno neque te novit neque gnatam tuam .
sections.translation: This Procurer ( pointing to the house of DORDALUS .) neither knows yourself nor your daughter .

sections.label: Section 1.3.132
sections.original: Me ut quisquam norit , nisi ille qui praebet cibum ?
sections.translation: How should any one know me , except him who finds me food ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.133
sections.original: Ita est . hoc tu mihi reperire argentum potes .
sections.translation: Such is the fact . This way you can find some money for me .

sections.label: Section 1.3.134
sections.translation: I ’ faith , I wish I could .

sections.label: Section 1.3.134b
sections.original: Tum tu me sine illam vendere .
sections.translation: Then do you allow me to sell her .

sections.label: Section 1.3.135
sections.translation: You to sell her ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.135b
sections.translation: Why no , I ’ ll depute another person to sell her , and to say that he is a foreigner ; since it isn ’ t six months since that Procurer removed hither from Megara [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.3.138b
sections.translation: The remnants are spoiling ; this , however , can be done afterwards .

sections.label: Section 1.3.139b
sections.translation: Do you understand on what terms it can ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.140
sections.original: numquam hercle hodie hic prius edes , ne frustra sis ,
sections.translation: Never , on my word , shall you eat here this day , so don ’ t be mistaken , before you declare to me that you ’ ll do this that I ’ m requesting ; and unless you bring your daughter with you hither at once as soon as you can , by my faith , I ’ ll cashier you from this squad . What now ? What ’ s the matter ? Why don ’ t you say what you will do ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.145
sections.original: Quaeso hercle me quoque etiam vende , si lubet ,
sections.translation: I ’ troth , prithee sell even myself as well , if you like , so long as you sell me with my stomach full .

sections.label: Section 1.3.146b
sections.translation: If you are going to do this , do it .

sections.label: Section 1.3.147
sections.translation: For my part , I ’ ll do what you desire ,
